lesson.menu_desc: Building rapport before the meeting starts -- open questions and follow-ups -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Small Talk Before Business -- Building Rapport
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Walyson Ginaldo Silva | Aula 18 -- Small Talk Before Business

listenings.text: Hi Walyson, this is Clara. I just landed in Santos for our meeting, and I am calling to break the ice a little before we sit down tomorrow. The flight was long, so I have a bit of jet lag, but I am fine. The weather here is beautiful, much warmer than back home. By the way, I had a great weekend before the trip; I went hiking, which is my favorite hobby. I hope the traffic from the airport will not be too bad. I think we will get along really well; I always like to build some rapport before we talk business. See you tomorrow. Have a good evening.

This is my note to prepare for the small talk before the meeting with Clara. My goal is to build rapport and make her feel welcome, because I am the host this time. First, I will ask open questions, not just yes or no questions. I will ask how her flight was and whether she has jet lag, so she can tell me more. Then I will find common ground; we both enjoy hiking, so that is a good topic. I will ask about her weekend and her hobbies, and I will follow up with what about you. I will mention the weather and the traffic, which are safe and easy topics. I want my guest to relax and feel that we get along before we talk business.

extra_audio.text: First, greet your guest warmly and welcome them as the host. Next, break the ice with a safe, easy topic, for example the weather or the trip. After that, ask open questions, not just yes or no questions, so the other person can tell you more. Then, listen and follow up with a question like what about you, and look for common ground such as a shared hobby. Finally, when you have built some rapport and you both get along, move gently from small talk to business.
